description: MySQL and MariaDB are relational database services. In labs and CTFs, they may reveal application databases, users, credentials, version details, and useful data after valid credentials are found.
why_prioritize: Database services can contain application users, password hashes, tokens, configuration data, and sensitive records.
The service version and authentication behavior can help you understand the backend environment.
Credentials found from web apps, FTP, SMB, NFS, or config files often work against database services in labs.

enumeration_steps.title: Check version and basic service information
enumeration_steps.reason: Version and protocol details help identify the database family and possible compatibility behavior.

enumeration_steps.title: Check whether anonymous or empty-password access exists
enumeration_steps.reason: Some lab databases allow weak or anonymous access, but this should be checked carefully and only in scope.

enumeration_steps.title: Use discovered credentials to connect
enumeration_steps.reason: Database credentials are commonly found inside web configuration files, backups, NFS exports, FTP files, or SMB shares.

enumeration_steps.title: List databases and useful tables after login
enumeration_steps.reason: After authentication, database structure helps you locate users, credentials, settings, or application data.

next_steps: Record where the credentials were found and what service they belong to.
Try them against MySQL only inside the authorized lab scope.
After login, list databases and identify application-specific names.
Check whether the same username appears in web apps, SSH, SMB, or FTP.
List databases and tables before dumping anything large.
Look for user, admin, settings, session, token, or config tables.
Check your privileges so you know what the account can read or modify.
Save important outputs neatly in your notes.
Identify username, email, password, hash, role, and status columns.
Check whether passwords are plaintext, hashed, or encoded.
Use recovered credentials carefully against related services in the lab.
Do not assume hashes are crackable; record format and context first.
Look for base URLs, upload paths, mail settings, debug flags, and API keys.
Connect settings to web paths or services found in the scan.
Check whether file paths reveal usernames or operating system details.
Use configuration clues to guide web and filesystem enumeration.
Search for known issues affecting the exact product and version.
Confirm the version through mysql-info or an authenticated query when possible.
Treat version information as a lead, not proof of vulnerability.
Continue checking credentials and exposed application data.

beginner_notes: Databases usually need credentials, so check web config files, backups, FTP, SMB, and NFS for passwords.
Do not run destructive SQL commands in labs unless the task explicitly asks for it.
List structure first, then query small amounts of useful data.
Take careful notes of database names, table names, columns, and where credentials came from.
